fund.name: HDFC Flexi Cap Fund - Growth Option - Direct Plan

allocations.rationale: This fund spreads money across large, mid, and small companies, giving steady growth over time. HDFC is a trusted fund house, and the fund has delivered strong 5-year returns with moderate risk. Good core holding for long-term wealth.

fund.name: Motilal Oswal Large and Midcap Fund - Direct Plan Growth
fund.fund_house: Motilal Oswal Asset Management Company Limited

allocations.rationale: Focuses on fast-growing mid-sized companies, which can deliver higher returns over long periods. Has outperformed its category with strong 3-year and 5-year returns. Adds growth potential to your portfolio.

fund.name: ICICI Prudential Equity & Debt Fund - Direct Plan - Growth
fund.fund_house: ICICI Prudential Asset Management Company Limited

allocations.rationale: Mixes stocks and bonds (debt) to reduce ups and downs. Gives better returns than savings accounts with lower risk than pure stock funds. Ideal for conservative investors who want growth with stability.

fund.name: Motilal Oswal Nifty Midcap 150 Index Fund - Direct Plan
fund.fund_house: Motilal Oswal Asset Management Company Limited

allocations.rationale: Tracks 150 mid-sized companies with low yearly fees (0.2%). Offers pure exposure to midcap growth at low cost. Adds diversification and boosts long-term returns.

plan.setup_phase:
Step 1: Download Groww or Kuvera app.
Step 2: Complete KYC (takes 5 minutes with PAN and Aadhaar).
Step 3: Open a 'Direct' mutual fund account.
Step 4: Start SIPs in these four funds with the amounts shown.
Step 5: Set a monthly reminder on your phone for SIP date.
review_checkpoints: After 6 months: Check if all SIPs are running smoothly.
After 1 year: Compare each fund’s return with its category average. If any fund is consistently underperforming, consider switching.
Every year: Review your risk comfort. As you gain experience, you may feel ready to adjust your mix.
plan.rebalancing_guidelines: Once a year, check your investment amounts. If one fund has grown much more than planned (e.g., more than 10% above target), sell some and buy more of the others to bring it back. Example: If midcap grows to 30% of your total, reduce it to 20% and add to flexi cap or hybrid.
plan.rationale: At 22, you have 38 years to grow your money. Even with a conservative nature, you can afford some risk for higher returns. This plan uses mostly equity funds for long-term growth but includes a hybrid fund to reduce volatility. It builds SIP discipline, supports retirement and home goals, and keeps things simple for a first-time investor. Since you're under the new tax regime, we avoid ELSS and use only equity-taxed funds for better post-tax returns.
risks: Stock markets can drop 20-30% in a bad year — your portfolio value will fall temporarily. This is normal and usually recovers over 2-3 years.
Mid and small-cap funds are more volatile — they grow faster but swing more.
Past returns don't guarantee future results. A fund that did well may slow down.
Inflation can erode returns if your portfolio doesn't grow faster than prices.
plan.disclaimer: For research/educational purposes only. Not certified financial advice.

sip_step_up.description: Increase SIP by 10% every year to match salary growth

allocation_schedule.trigger: After 10 years or when corpus reaches ₹10 lakh
After 20 years or when corpus reaches ₹50 lakh
After 30 years or when corpus reaches ₹2 crore

aps.reasoning:
passive_instrument_fraction: The equity allocation consists of three actively managed funds (HDFC Flexi Cap, Motilal Oswal Large & Midcap, ICICI Prudential Equity & Debt) and one passive fund (Motilal Oswal Nifty Midcap 150 Index Fund). The index fund constitutes 10% of the total portfolio, but within the equity sleeve (90% of total), it is 1/9 ≈ 11.1%. Hence, the passive fraction in equity is ~11.1%, scoring ~0.1.

turnover_score: Rebalancing is recommended annually or when allocations deviate by more than 10%, and review is yearly. No frequent trading or tactical shifts are suggested. This reflects moderate turnover, leaning passive. Score: 0.75.

cost_emphasis_score: The plan highlights low expense ratio of the index fund (0.2%, though listed as 0.0 possibly in error) and implies cost efficiency as a benefit, but does not make cost the primary selection criterion across all funds. Other selections emphasize past returns and fund house reputation. Moderate cost focus: score 0.6.

research_vs_cost_score: Fund rationales are based on performance, category fit, and fund house trust, not deep research into individual stocks or manager skill. However, active funds are selected for their outperformance, implying some research-based conviction. Still, no detailed analysis of moats, PEG ratios, or management quality. Leans active but not deeply researched. Score: 0.2.

time_horizon_alignment_score: The plan is designed for a 38-year horizon, with SIP discipline, step-up, and long-term compounding. Review and rebalancing are infrequent. Strong long-term orientation: score 0.9.

portfolio_activeness_score: Three of four funds are actively managed (Flexi Cap, Large & Midcap, Aggressive Hybrid), with only one true index fund. No risk metrics (beta, tracking error, etc.) are provided, but category labels and rationales suggest active management. The portfolio is tilted toward active funds with performance chasing, indicating low passivity. Score: 0.2.

pqs.reasoning: The plan scores highly on goal alignment as it addresses the investor's long-term horizon (38 years), retirement and home purchase goals, and need for SIP discipline, while acknowledging their conservative risk appetite by including a 20% allocation to Aggressive Hybrid funds. Diversification is strong across market caps (flexi cap, large & midcap, midcap index) and fund houses, though midcap exposure (30% combined) may be slightly high for a conservative investor. Risk-return is appropriate given the long horizon; the rationale justifies equity bias and includes realistic return projections (18.7% base) with downside scenarios. Internal consistency is excellent: the rationale, allocations, rebalancing, and review checkpoints align coherently. Tax efficiency is optimal: the investor is in the 'new_regime', and the plan correctly avoids ELSS (no 80C benefit), uses only equity-taxed funds (all holdings are equity or aggressive hybrid with >65% equity), and explicitly states this tax-aware rationale. No slab-taxed debt funds are used, maximizing post-tax returns under current Indian tax rules.
